coredata.dc:description: In recent years, TiO2-based UV photodetectors (UVPDs) have gained widespread attention owing to their abundant raw materials and low synthesis cost. However, the low responsivity, slow response speed and high-power consumption limit the application of these UVPDs. In this study, p-n heterojunction self-powered UVPDs consisting of n-type coal-based carbon quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 nanorod arrays (TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs) and p-type poly(2, 2′:5′, 2′'-trithiophene) (PTTh) are reported. The structure, morphology and photoresponse properties of the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunctions are methodically investigated. These results indicate that the sensitization of C-CQDs has a significant impact on the electrical and optical properties of p-n heterojunction UVPDs. The performance study results of UVPDs at different optical power densities show that the device is able to accurately detect UV light of specific wavelengths, and the device achieved a maximum responsivity (R) (1.45 mA/cm2) and a high detectivity (D*) (4.94 × 1010 Jones) when the light intensity is 0.35 mW/cm2, while the rise and fall times shortened to 91.2 ms and 3.19 ms, respectively. These indicate that TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh achieves the high performance self-powered UV light detection. This work offers theoretical support and technical route for TiO2 based self-powered UVPDs.

head.ce:title: Coal-based carbon quantum dots-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterostructure for self-powered UV detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, TiO2-based UV photodetectors (UVPDs) have gained widespread attention owing to their abundant raw materials and low synthesis cost. However, the low responsivity, slow response speed and high-power consumption limit the application of these UVPDs. In this study, p-n heterojunction self-powered UVPDs consisting of n-type coal-based carbon quantum dot-sensitized TiO2 nanorod arrays (TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs) and p-type poly(2, 2′:5′, 2′'-trithiophene) (PTTh) are reported. The structure, morphology and photoresponse properties of the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunctions are methodically investigated. These results indicate that the sensitization of C-CQDs has a significant impact on the electrical and optical properties of p-n heterojunction UVPDs. The performance study results of UVPDs at different optical power densities show that the device is able to accurately detect UV light of specific wavelengths, and the device achieved a maximum responsivity (R) (1.45 mA/cm2) and a high detectivity (D*) (4.94 × 1010 Jones) when the light intensity is 0.35 mW/cm2, while the rise and fall times shortened to 91.2 ms and 3.19 ms, respectively.
These indicate that TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh achieves the high performance self-powered UV light detection. This work offers theoretical support and technical route for TiO2 based self-powered UVPDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Self-powered photodetectors present various attractive features and have become one of the development directions for next-generation photodetectors since they exhibit low power consumption and no external power supply [1–3]. And the self-powered UVPDs have gained much attention owing to its application in environmental monitoring, flame warning, and optical communication [4–6]. Many self-powered UVPDs based on the photovoltaic effect generated by Schottky junctions and p-n junctions have been reported in recent years [7,8]. Compared with Schottky junction devices, p-n junction self-powered UVPDs have received a lot of attention because of its fast response and considerable detectivity [9,10]. Therefore, some p-n heterojunctions have been used to explore self-powered UVPDs, such as NiO/Ga2O3 [6], ZnOEP/GaN[11], n-Ga2O3/p-CuSCN[12], and TiO2/P3HT[13]. Nevertheless, the performance of these self-powered UVPDs still needs improvement in the present commercial requirements. Thus, there is an urgent to develop self-powered UVPDs with superior performance, low power consumption, sustainable, long-term operation and maintenance-free advantages.

TiO2, as a typical broad gap n-type material, has been successfully applied in self-powered UVPDs. Cao and coworker[14] prepared TiO2 NRs/NiO p-n heterojunction, which constructed a strong built-in electric field and realized high-performance self-powered UV detection. Zheng et al.[15] have demonstrated the MgO/TiO2 heterojunction to improve the responsivity (29.97 A/W), and its rising time of 0.02 s. Nonetheless, the TiO2-based self-powered UVPDs has been suffering from low carrier separation efficiency[16–18]. Therefore, it is a promising method to improve the properties of TiO2-based self-powered photodetector via finding suitable p-type materials and using appropriate materials to modify the heterojunction. In the past few years, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a size of only a few nanometers, have gained widespread attention owing to their superior optoelectronic properties and powerful absorption in the UV zone, especially in the application of photoelectric devices, solar cells and light-emitting diodes (LEDs)[19,20]. For example, Goswami et al.[21] used GQDs as sensitizer to sensitize ZnO nanorods and prepared GQDs sensitized ZnO/GaN heterojunctions to detect UV light, the results indicate that the performance of the device is improved by ∼ 265% after sensitization. Kalytchuk and coworker[22] synthesized purple-emissive carbon dots and used it to sensitize the Si based photodetector, they found the performance of the sensitized device is improved by 203.8% while realizing UV detection. Nevertheless, the modification of TiO2 with quantum dots derived from coal is rarely reported in the field of photodetector. In addition to improving photodetector performance by introducing new elements into TiO2, p-type materials that form a good p-n junction with n-type TiO2 are also crucial[23,24]. In this scenario, the construction of superior p-n heterojunctions between TiO2: CQDs and adequate p-type materials will bring new hope for high-performance self-powered UVPDs. Polyterthiophene (PTTh) is a typical p-type material, and due to its high hole transport ability and large optical absorption bandwidth, it has been broadly used in photovoltaic devices, light-emitting diodes and other fields[25,26]. Since the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of PTTh is −5.3 eV, the p-n heterojunction composed of PTTh and TiO2 NRs: C-CQDs (i.e. TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh) is expected to provide an opportunity for self-powered UVPDs of superior performance.

In this study, TiO2 NRs and C-CQDs are respectively prepared by hydrothermal method and hydrogen peroxide oxidation method, and then TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunction is prepared by a combination of physical spin-coating and electrochemical deposition methods. And the structure, morphology and photoresponse properties of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunction are systematically studied.

The sensitization of TiO2 NRs by C-CQDs forms a new interface in the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction, which facilitates the separation and transport of photogenerated carriers. Thus, TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction UVPDs is expected to come true self-powered UV photodetection with superior performance.

This study not only provides a technical route for the research of self-powered UVPDs with superior performance, but also provides a good direction for the comprehensive utilization of coal resources.
All chemical reagents, materials and characterizations methods are detailed in the supporting information.
TiO2 NRs is synthesized according to Ref.[24]. Specific procedures are given in Supporting Information.

The coal-based carbon quantum dots (C-CQDs) ware prepared by a chemical oxidation method according to Ref.[27]. Briefly, firstly, the coal sample was fully ground and screened through 200 mesh to obtain fine coal powder, then the coal powder (5 g) was put into a porcelain boat and conduct air pre-oxidation in a muffle furnace, keep at 240 °C for 4 h, and then cooled to room temperature, and stored in bags for later use. Take 0.5 g of pre-oxidized coal powder and add it to a double-necked flask, then 40 ml of H2O2 (30 wt%) was added and keep it at 90 °C for 3 h to gain a carbon quantum dot solution. After that, the solution was transferred to dialysis bag (1000 Da) after 0.22 μm microporous membrane for 36 h. The solution after dialysis was freeze-dried to obtain coal-based carbon quantum dot powders (C-CQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TiO2 NRs: C-CQDs/PTTh composite
ce:section.ce:para: The above-prepared C-CQDs powder was prepared into a 0.5 mg/ml C-CQDs aqueous solution, and then the C-CQDs solution was spin-coated on the TiO2 NRs via spin coating 5 times, keeping the spin-coating rate at 1500 r/min for 30 s[28,29]. Then, the TiO2 substrate spin-coated with C-CQDs was kept in a vacuum drying oven at 110 ℃ for 30 min, and the sample was denoted as TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs. The TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunction was fabricated by depositing a layer polymer on the prepared TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs surface via electrochemical polymerization[30]. The electrochemical polymerization is taken place in a mixed solution of DCM/MeCN (7/3, volume rate) with monomer (2 mM) and TBAPF6 (0.1 M) as electrolyte. Polymer films were gained through CV technology with a range (0–1.3 V) of control voltage and a scan rate (100 mV/s). In the end, the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh composite was rinsed in a mixed solvent of DCM and MeCN.

The flowchart of material preparation and the preparation process of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction UVPDs are shown in scheme 1 . The TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction with TiO2 NRs as the base material is used as the working electrode, and another piece of FTO glass of the same size is used as the counter electrode. The TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction UVPDs were obtained by face-to-face bonding between the junction side and the conductive surface of the blank FTO glass.

To demonstrate the successful preparation of C-CQDs, the morphology, crystal structures and UV–vis absorption of the as-synthesized C-CQDs samples are characterized. The TEM image of C-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a). The distribution of C-CQDs is relatively uniform and granular, and most particles are distributed in the range of 3.8–8 nm with an average diameter of 6.16 nm. In addition, it can be seen from the inset of Fig. 1(a) that the prepared C-CQDs have a high crystallinity with a crystalline surface spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to (100) facets graphitic carbon[27]. The XRD pattern ( Fig. 1 (b)) of C-CQDs shows a wide diffraction peak at about 2θ = 23°, which is attributed to the diffraction peak of amorphous carbon, and there are no other impurity peaks, which is consistent with previous reported literature[31]. The UV–vis spectrum of the as-prepared C-CQDs ( Fig. 1(c)) shows a powerful absorption peak at about 220 nm and a broad shoulder peak at 310 nm. This respectively is caused by the π-π* transition of the C = C bond and the n-π* transition of the C = O bond. And it is consistent with the values reported[27]. The above results indicate that C-CQDs were successfully synthesized.

The functional groups and chemical compositions of the C-CQDs are further analyzed by FT-IR and XPS, as shown in Figure S1. The FT-IR spectrum of C-CQDs (
Figure S1
(a)) shows a broad peak at 3428 cm−1 attributed to O-H stretching vibration, and absorption peaks at 1450 cm−1 and 884 cm−1 attributed to backbone vibration of C = C and C-H bonding in aromatic hydrocarbons, respectively. In addition, there is a C = O vibration absorption band at 1708 cm−1 and a C-O stretching vibration at 1614–1250 cm−1 [32]. The results suggest that C-CQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. The XPS full-scan spectrum and the high-resolution spectra of corresponding elements are presented in Figure S1
(b-d). The two signal peaks at 284.8 and 531.3 eV in the full-scan spectrum (
Figure S1
(b)) are attributed to the presence of C and O elements[32,33]. After peak fitting, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (
Figure S1
(c)) show three peaks at 284.8, 286.6, and 288.7 eV that can be attributed to C = C/C-C, C-O, and C = O, respectively. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s shows two peaks at 531.3 and 532.8 eV (
Figure S1
(c)), corresponding to C = O/C-OH and C-O, respectively. The analysis results of XPS spectra showed that the prepared C-CQDs are rich in C, O elements, which further confirmed the above FT-IR analysis results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical synthesis process of PTTh is shown in Fig. 2 (a). The electrochemical polymerization is carried out under the condition of the voltage range (0–1.3 V) at the specific scan rate (100 mV/s). And TBAPF6 (0.1 M) is used as the electrolyte, the electrolyte is consisted of DCM and MeCN (7:3 VDCM/VMeCN), and the concentration of TTh is 2 mM. PTTh has an initial oxidation potential of 1.01 V from Fig. 2(a). The initial cycle mainly is attribute to the nucleation effect of the PTTh. From the Fig. 2(a), the current intensity of the monomer slowly increases while the number of cycles rising, which indicates that PTTh polymer is triumphantly grown on the surface of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs substrate. Owing to the presence of larger conjugated thiophene units in the polymerization system (e.g., 1.0/0.8 V (tetra thiophene), etc.), the new redox peaks appear at lower potential[34], which successfully indicated the occurrence of electrochemical polymerization. After five cycles of polymerization, TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunctions are successfully fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure and optical properties of prepared materials
ce:section.ce:para: It can be obtained from Fig. 2(b) that the XRD diffraction patterns of all samples have peaks at 26.69°, 37.96°, 51.73°, 61.84° and 65.69°, which correspond to the characteristic diffraction peaks of FTO conductive glass. The PTTh exists two feeble diffraction peaks (at 2θ = 32° and 42°), which are put down to the fact that the characteristic peaks of polymer[26]. For the TiO2 NRs, except the FTO substrate, all diffraction peaks are consistent with the reported literature[24], and there are no other crystal forms (anatase, brookite) of TiO2, nor other impurity peaks. And the diffraction peaks at 36.27°, 54.65°, 62.88° and 70.04° of TiO2 NRs are attributed to the (101), (211), (002) and (112) planes of tetragonal rutile TiO2. In addition, the shape of the diffraction peaks of TiO2 NRs is sharp, indicating that the prepared TiO2 NRs has high crystallinity[24]. At the same time, it can also be gained from the diffraction patterns that the diffraction peak of (002) crystal plane for TiO2 is higher, which indicates that TiO2 NRs grows perpendicular to FTO along the orientation of (001). The diffraction peaks of PTTh and rutile TiO2 are observed in the diffraction patterns of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh composites. The diffraction peak of TiO2 NRs at (101) is stronger, but all diffraction peaks in TiO2 NRs: C-CQDs/PTTh composites are significantly weaker than that of pure TiO2 NRs at 101. The reason for the phenomenon is attributed to the fact that the polymer films block the penetration of X-rays. Thus, the diffraction peak intensity becomes weak. In addition, compared to TiO2 NRs, the C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction material shows no extra diffraction peaks, it indicates that the crystal structure of TiO2 NRs does not change after sensitization. There is no present the characteristic diffraction peak of C-CQDs in the composites, which indicates that TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs are completely covered by polymer or the characteristic diffraction peaks of C-CQDs are covered by strong diffraction peaks of PTTh and TiO2 NRs.

The UV–vis spectra of materials are shown in Fig. 2(c). The TiO2 NRs shows a powerful UV absorption characteristics[35]. The UV–vis spectrum of PTTh shows a broad absorption in the UV–vis region (350–600 nm), which is in accord with the reported literature[25,26,36]. For composites, the UV–vis spectrum of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs is similar to that of TiO2 NRs, with strong absorption in the UV region. For the C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction composites, the characteristic absorption peaks of TiO2 NRs and PTTh can be observed, indicating the successful preparation of the heterojunction composites. Furthermore, the change of the material's bandgap value is explored after the introduction of C-CQDs. By calculation, the Tauc plots of TiO2 NRs and composites are shown in Fig. 2(c). And the direct band gap value of TiO2 NRs is 3.11 eV, which is consistent with the rutile-phase TiO2 NRs synthesized in previous studies[14]. The band gap values of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs and TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh are 3.07 eV and 2.16 eV, respectively, which indicate that there is a good match between TiO2 NRs, C-CQDs and PTTh chains in the heterojunction interface[37]. This is beneficial to improve the performance of UVPDs.

The existence of C-CQDs in the composites is confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectra of the TiO2 NRs, PTTh and composites are studied as shown in Fig. 2(d). The Raman shifts at 450 and 630 cm−1 can be put down to the characteristic spectral peaks of rutile TiO2 NRs, corresponding to previously reported[38]. The Raman spectrum of PTTh has scattering peaks at 696.5, 1045.8, and 1453 cm−1, which are consistent with literature reports[26]. And the composites can detect the scattering peaks of TiO2 NRs and PTTh. In the Raman spectra of the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs, the two peaks with Raman shifts at ∼ 1387.5 and ∼ 1608.3 cm−1 severally correspond to the D and G bands of carbon in C-CQDs. Furthermore, the ID/IG ratio in Raman spectroscopy is usually used to judge the degree of graphitization. The ID/IG value of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs is 0.99, indicating that C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs have a higher degree of graphitization[39]. For the C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh composites, the Raman shifts at ∼ 1503 and ∼ 1453 cm−1 are put down to the symmetric Cα = Cβ in PTTh[40]. However, the characteristic peaks of C-CQDs are not observed in composites, mainly because the strong band of PTTh almost overlapped with the G and D bands of C-CQDs.

The surface morphologies of materials are characterized via SEM technique, as shown in Fig. 3
(a–d). The top view of the TiO2 NRs indicates that the TiO2 NRs exhibit a tetrahedral morphology with regular morphology. It can also be seen from the cross-section view that there is a certain gap between each TiO2 NRs, it is beneficial for the transport of photogenerated carriers[41]. The average diameter and length of the TiO2 NRs are respectively about 100 nm and 2.69 μm via the hydrothermal method [24]. Afterwards, the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs is covered with a layer of polymer film through electrochemical method. It can be gained from the Fig. 3 (c, d) that the polymer film is evenly distributed. The tight bonding between the polymer films and C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs guarantee a better contact, and takes shape internal electric field helpful to separation of photogenerated carries[41]. In a word, the morphological features of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh are beneficial for the performance of photodetectors.

EDS and Mapping are usually used to study the chemical composition and elemental distribution of materials, the elemental EDS and Mapping analysis of the top view and the cross-section view of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh are shown in Fig. 3 and Figure S2 (Supporting Information). The top-view elemental EDS analysis results show the existence of Ti, O, S, and C elements in the material. The Mapping analysis results of the top view and the cross-sectional view both show that the distribution of each element in the prepared material is relatively uniform, which provides the possibility for the superior performance of the heterojunction composites. It also further illustrates the successful preparation of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction composites.

Commonly, the chemical composition and oxidation state of prepared materials are studied through the XPS. Fig. 4 (a) shows the XPS survey of the C-CQDs sensitized heterojunction material, which shows the presence of C, S, O, and Ti elements in the composites. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s ( Fig. 4(b)) for TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction materials. The three splitting peaks at 284.1 eV, 284.8 eV and 286.1 eV are attributed to C = C, C–C and C–O bond, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s ( Fig. 4(c)) in TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction materials is deconvoluted into three peaks, severally corresponding to 530.2 eV, 532.2 and 533.24 eV. It is ascribed that the presence of Ti-O bonds, O-H bonds and C-O bonds in the composites[42]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of S2p ( Fig. 4(d)) appear two peaks corresponding to 163.8 eV (S2p3/2) and 164.9 eV (S2p1/2), which are belonged to the spin splitting doublet of the sulfur atom[36]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of Ti2p ( Fig. 4(e)) peak at 459.5 eV and 465.5 eV, severally corresponding to Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2, indicating that Ti4+ of rutile phase TiO2 in the composites is in a normal chemical state. The above results further demonstrate the successful preparation of C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction UVPDs.
ce:section.ce:para:
In photovoltaic mode, the UV photodetection performance of C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction is investigated. The effect of C-CQDs sensitization on the optoelectronic properties of the as-prepared device is analyzed, in which the device exhibits low dark current and enhances UV light-generated carriers. The current − voltage (I − V) curves of the hybrid heterojunction in the dark and different light intensities (0.35–2 mW/cm2) are displayed in Fig. 5
. The Fig. 5(b) is an enlarged view of I-V when applying a bias voltage in the range of −0.3–0.3 V, and Fig. 5(c) is the logarithmic I-V curves. The device exhibits a dark current of less than 15 μA at 1 V. And the device shows a continuous increase in the photocurrent (Iph = Ilight-Idark) with the increasing intensity of UV light. This is mainly due to the increased rate of electron-hole pair generation under UV light irradiation of higher power density[43]. In addition, compared to TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction UVPDs ( Figure S3
.), the TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction UVPDs sensitized via C-CQDs has a higher photocurrent in the UV light intensity of 0.35mW /cm2 at 365 nm. And the photocurrent value of C-CQDs sensitized devices exceeds 310% under −1 V bias. Moreover, it can be clearly observed from Fig. 5(a) that the I-V curves of the hybrid heterojunction are nonlinear and asymmetric, suggesting that the device has good rectification characteristics, which also indicates that the prepared photodetector has a superior p-n heterojunction[37]. The short-circuit current (Ik) is 0.299 μA, and the open circuit voltage (Uo) is − 0.063 V for the hybrid p-n heterojunction UVPDs under the 0.35 mW/cm2 at 365 nm from the Fig. 5(b). And the Uo and Ik gradually increase with the increase of light intensity. When the light intensity increases to 2 mW/cm2, the Uo reaches −0.096 V and Ik increases to 0.749 μA. The generation of these potentials is mainly attributed to the p-n heterojunction formed via n-type TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs and p-type PTTh. The above results illustrate that the C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh hybrid heterojunctions displays excellent photovoltaic effect. The internal electric field will generate a tractive force to suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, enabling self-powered UV light detection. These results are attributed to the sensitization of TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunctions by C-CQDs, which not only enhances UV absorption but also enhances the concentration of photogenerated carriers, promising high-performance self-powered detection.

In order to further investigate the effect of C-CQDs on the performance of UVPDs, the time-dependent transient photoresponse (I-T) curves ( Fig. 6
(a, b)) of TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction sensitized by C-CQDs are studied under self-powered mode and variable UV light intensity (0.35 to 2 mW/cm2) at 365 nm. It can be obtained from the figure that the current of the hybrid p-n heterojunction UVPDs increases rapidly in the conditions of the UV light source turns on, and the conditions of the UV light source turns off, the current decreases rapidly. In addition, the C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh p-n heterojunction UVPDs exhibit a stable time-dependent transient photoresponse, and the photocurrent gradually increases with increasing light intensity.

Usually, responsivity (R), detectivity (D*), and response time (τ) are the key figure-of-merit parameters for evaluating photovoltaic photodetectors, where R is the ratio of photocurrent (Iph) to optical power density (P) and effective illumination area (S) of the photodetector, which reflects the response efficiency of the prepared device to the optical signal. The D* mainly reflects the detection ability of the prepared device for weak signals in a noisy environment. The greater the detectivity, the stronger the detection ability. The corresponding responsivity (R) and detectivity (D*) are calculated as (1) and (2), respectively[9,13,44]:

In the formula, Ilight represents the maximum current under UV light, Idark represents the dark current under dark conditions, and P, S, and e respectively represent the light intensity, the effective illumination area of the prepared device, and the elemental charge.

Furthermore, the response time is divided into a rise time (τr) and a fall time (τf). The rise time (τr) is decided to be the time it takes for the photocurrent to rise from 10% to 90% of the maximum value under illumination, likewise, the fall time (τf) is decided to be the time required for the photocurrent to drop from 90% to 10% of the maximum value[13,45,46].

The I-T plots of the TiO2 NRs/PTTh p-n heterojunction self-powered UVPDs in the light intensity of 0.35 mW/cm2 are displayed in Figure S4 . It can be obtained from the Figure S4
(a) that the photocurrent and the dark current of the fabricated device are 7.34nA, and −1.37nA, respectively. The τr and τf respectively are 0.39 and 0.35 s. And the R and D* respectively are 0.053 mA/W and 1.44 × 109 Jones. From the Figure S4
(b), the device of TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction has good stability. However, after C-CQDs sensitization, the transient response curve of the device is displayed in Fig. 6 (a, b). Compared with before sensitization, the C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh p-n heterojunction UVPDs exhibit excellent UV detection performance under the light intensity of 0.35 mW/cm2 at 365 nm, and the photocurrent is increased by at least 22 times to 162.7 nA, the τr and τf are shortened to 91.2 ms and 3.19 ms, respectively, and R and D* severally reach 1.45 mA/W and 4.95 × 1010 Jones. Meanwhile, the device also shows excellent stability. As shown in Figure S5, even under the light intensity of 2 mW/cm2, the photocurrent of the device remains basically unchanged after 20 cycles, achieving self-powered UV detection with superior performance.

In addition, the I-T curves of the C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh UVPDs under different light intensity are investigated, as displayed in Fig. 6(b). The TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunction UVPDs show highly stable and reproducible features. And the relationship between light intensity and photocurrent is shown in Fig. 6(c). As the light intensity increases from 0.35 to 2 mW cm2, it can be obviously obtained that the photocurrent increases almost linearly. This linear change of the photodetector indicates that the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunction UVPDs can be used for accurate UV light measurements. This also means that the efficiency of photogenerated carriers is proportional to the flux of absorbed photons, indicating that the photoresponse of the TiO2 NRs: C-CQDs/PTTh UVPDs is mainly determined via the drift of photogenerated carriers[16].

The calculated values of R and D* under different light intensity are described in Fig. 6 (d). It can be gained from the figure that the values of R and D* increase with the decrease of the light intensity. When the light intensity is 0.35 mW/cm2, the responsivity reaches 1.45 mA/W, and the detectivity reaches 4.95 × 1010 Jones, which are mainly caused by the carrier screening effect of the active layer[47].

We compared the C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh p-n heterojunction devices with reported related photodetectors, and the results are shown in Table 1 . The photodetectors decorated with CQDs or GQDs exhibit superior performance, with large responsivity and detectivity. However, there are few reports on the heterojunction self-powered photodetectors composed of n- TiO2 and p- polymer, and the reported photodetectors also exist some problems such as low responsivity, low detectivity and long response time etc. There are few reports that the CQDs sensitized TiO2 to enhance the performance of TiO2-based self-powered UVPDs. Although the performance of TiO2 or ZnO photodetectors modified via CQDs has been improved, the detection under high bias voltage is not conducive to energy saving. Therefore, the self-powered UVPDs with C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh heterojunction have great advantages. The performance of the UVPDs has been improved, not only the responsivity and detectivity are improved, but also the response speed is obviously accelerated, basically realizing high-performance self-powered UV light detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The operating mechanism of the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh heterojunction self-powered UVPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Operating mechanism of the C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh p-n heterojunction UVPDs is unambiguously elaborated in Fig. 7 (a). A p-n heterojunction is formed at the interface of C-CQDs-sensitized TiO2 NRs and PTTh, resulting in a depletion region and internal electric field. Once 365 nm light is irradiated onto the self-powered UVPDs, the photoexcitation process will generate a large number of electron-hole pairs, which will increase the carrier concentration in the TiO2 NRs. Due to the presence of the built-in electric field, electron-hole pairs are effectively separated, enabling self-powered detection. The band diagram of hybrid heterojunction UVPDs is shown in Fig. 7(b). And these values of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of materials are obtained from published papers[25,49,54]. The C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs are prone to generate photogenerated carriers in the sufficient energy UV light, and the photogenerated electrons are motivated to the CB of the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs. Moreover, compared to the VB of TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs, the HOMO level of organic polymers is lower, so the photogenerated holes are easily moved to the HOMO of polymer, then transfer to FTO electrode. In the same way, most of the excited electrons in the TiO2 NRs:CQDs reach the top of the CB and then transfer to another FTO electrode, thus, the photocurrent is generated in the external circuit to realize self-powered detection. Additionally, the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunction generates more photogenerated electron-hole pairs owing to the powerful absorption of C-CQDs in the UV region and the formation of a good energy level matching relationship between TiO2 NRs sensitized with C-CQDs and polymer PTTh. However, the presence of depletion layer between p-n junctions inhibits the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, and electrons move toward the TiO2 NRs side and holes move toward the PTTh side of the polymer, resulting in multiple electrons on the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs side and multiple holes on the PTTh side in the hybrid heterojunction, thus achieving efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The photocurrent is increased and the response speed is accelerated, and realized the high performance self-powered UV light detection.

In summary, the self-powered UVPDs with superior performance based on TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh p-n heterojunctions are successfully prepared via combining chemical and physical spin-coating methods. It is found that the sensitization of TiO2 NRs with C-CQDs are beneficial to improve the carrier transportation in the hybrid heterojunction, prolongs the carrier lifetime, and enhances the responsivity and the photocurrent of UVPDs. Specifically, when the light intensity is 0.35 mW/cm2, the responsivity (R) and detectivity (D*) of the TiO2 NRs:C-CQDs/PTTh device are 1.45 mA/W and 4.5 × 1010 Jones, respectively, and the response time shortened to 91.2/3.19 ms. The research results of UVPDs at different light intensities show that the R and D* values increase with decreasing light intensity and the photocurrent value increases linearly with increasing light intensity, which also indicates that C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs/PTTh p-n heterojunction UVPDs can accurately detect UV light. Therefore, the present work provides new ideas on how C-CQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs can effectively improve the performance of organic–inorganic p-n heterojunction UVPDs and also opens up a new way for the comprehensive utilization of coal resources.
